summary: DHH highlights X (formerly Twitter) engineering team's announcement of 60% cloud cost reduction by moving infrastructure on-premises. He frames this as validation of the cloud exit movement, noting X achieved these savings while simultaneously reducing staff from 8,000 to under 2,000. DHH argues this proves that large organizations are massively overspending on cloud services. He predicts CFOs and investors will take notice, potentially pushing cloud exit into the mainstream.
key_points: X reduced monthly cloud costs by 60%, cloud data storage by 60%, and cloud data processing costs by 75% by moving to on-prem infrastructure
These savings were achieved with a workforce reduced to a quarter of its original size, undermining the argument that cloud exit requires more staff
Based on reported $100M/year AWS spend, X is on track to save $60M annually from cloud exit alone
DHH deliberately sidesteps the political debate around Musk to focus purely on the infrastructure economics
He positions X's results as a signal that cloud exit is about to go mainstream across large corporations
money_quotes: Musk has taken a cleaver to the costs and complexity at X. It hasn't always been pretty, but it sure has been effective.

Not only has the site stayed up, despite hysteric proclamations that it would crater soon after his personnel changes, but X has been able to increase the pace of experimentation and feature introductions at the same time.
However you're aligned, you'll find a chart to support your position that X is either thriving or has one foot in the grave.
Reduced the monthly cloud cost by 60%(!!) by moving work out of the cloud and onto their own servers.
What's even more impressive than the fact they've been able to cut their cloud bill so much, so quickly is that they've done it with a team a quarter the size of what it was.

If it's possible, and Musk is busy proving that it is, to run an operation like X with a quarter the staff, and STILL profit massively from exiting the cloud, you know there are gigantious savings waiting to be unlocked.
The #CloudExit might well be at the cusp of going mainstream. Have you run your numbers?
key_insight: X's 60% cloud cost reduction achieved with a fraction of its former workforce proves that cloud exit can deliver massive savings even under constrained resources, signaling a potential mainstream shift away from cloud dependence.
